original_abstract: South Africa possesses a profound legacy of prophetic preaching, exemplified by figures such as Desmond Tutu who challenged the structural injustices of the past. However, in the post-apartheid era, the nation faces a burgeoning crisis of inequality and social exclusion: staggering poverty affecting nearly half the population. This article identifies the essential conditions for revitalizing prophetic preaching within this contemporary landscape. Drawing on social analysis and qualitative research into the experiences of the poor, the study argues that effective prophetic witness requires a deep solidarity with the marginalized, a nuanced homiletical understanding of the prophetic genre as distinct from everyday preaching, and the robust support of local congregations and the broader ecumenical church. In an age marked by corruption and the self-enrichment of the elite, the author suggests that the church must rediscover its voice to offer both a critique of current social failures and a vision of hope for a more just society.
humanized_abstract: One of the rich legacies that South Africa enjoys is that of powerful and prophetic preaching. This legacy includes notable church leaders such as the arch bishop Desmond Tutu, in their critique of the apartheid structures of inequality and injustice. Today many years later, South Africa continues to grapple with the inequality and the exclusion that characterises the huge and tragic poverty that afflicts over forty percent of our people. Based on insights from the social sciences and what we have learnt from the qualitative research we have done with poor people and communities, this article explores three conditions that need to be met in order to revive a truly prophetic kind of preaching and practice among the people of God, not only in South Africa but elsewhere in the world. These are firstly what we call ‘prophetic stance’ or ‘attitude’ toward the poor and to the challenge of poverty and exclusion that the poor represent. Secondly, there is what we call the homiletical ‘form’ of what constitutes a truly prophetic message and discourse which is quite different to ordinary ordinary preaching. Thirdly there is the need for a supportive and prophetic kind of community which will afford space for truly prophetic preaching and practice to flourish both within the Church and within the overall ecumenical body of Christ in the world. The church in our time lives in an era in which inequality, corruption and grandstanding and personal enrichment by the elite abound. If the church is to make sense in this time, she must find her voice to critique this inequality in the world and in our societal structures, and at the same time to speak a word of hope, promising a better life to our people.
